ShopFast — Online Retail Platform

Generated: 2026-07-21 11:33 UTC Methodologies: STRIDE, LINDDUN, PASTA LLM-enhanced: No (not requested)

System Description

Browser and mobile clients talk to an API gateway, which fans out to an auth service, an orders service backed by PostgreSQL, and an external payment provider. An internal admin portal manages catalog and refunds.

Severity Overview

Counts below are findings — threats proven by your model. Generic “standard checks” are listed lower down and not counted here.

Critical

92 findings — 225 rule-based, 0 LLM-enhanced, 30 cross-boundary  ·  133 standard checks (not counted).

🩺 Model health — 1 auto-resolved

  • ⚠ 1 component(s) have an unrecognized type (Payment Provider); they render but only attract generic threats.

Data-flow overview

ShopFast — Online Retail Platform has 9 components and 8 data flows across 4 trust zones. Requests enter at Web Frontend, API Gateway and Admin Portal, data is stored in Orders DB (Postgres). 5 trust-boundary crossings, 2 unencrypted flows, the highest-risk component is API Gateway (16 critical findings).

Entry points: Web Frontend, API Gateway, Admin PortalData stores: Orders DB (Postgres)External deps: —Boundary crossings: 5Unencrypted flows: 2
FlowWhy it's riskySeverity
API Gateway → Orders Serviceunencrypted; crosses a trust boundary; no/weak authenticationCritical
Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres)unencrypted; crosses a trust boundary; no/weak authenticationCritical
Customer Browser → Web Frontendcrosses a trust boundary; no/weak authenticationCritical
Mobile App → API Gatewaycrosses a trust boundary; no/weak authenticationCritical
API Gateway → Auth Servicecrosses a trust boundary; no/weak authenticationCritical
Web Frontend → API Gatewayno/weak authenticationCritical
Admin Portal → API Gatewayno/weak authenticationCritical
Orders Service → Payment Providerno/weak authenticationHigh
Risk hotspots: API Gateway (16C/4H) · Orders Service (13C/0H) · Web Frontend (10C/3H) · Auth Service (9C/0H)
⚠ Treat as unverified: 8 flows with no/weak authentication; 2 unencrypted flows.

🚧 Untrusted-Input Boundary Crossings

Flows where untrusted (or less-trusted) input crosses into an internal trust zone. These are the highest-priority validation points in the system — every byte that enters here must be treated as hostile until proven otherwise.

No untrusted-input boundary crossings detected. Either there are no internal zones defined, or no flows enter them from less-trusted zones.

Trust Boundary Analysis

2 components 0 ingress flows 2 egress flows 0 cross-boundary threats

  • Mobile App → API Gateway (—) — ['HTTPS'], auth: none, encrypted

🛡 DMZ (public-facing)

3 components 2 ingress flows 2 egress flows 12 cross-boundary threats
Contains: Web Frontend (webapp), API Gateway (api), Admin Portal (webapp)
⬇ Ingress (data entering this zone):
  • Customer Browser → Web Frontend (—) — ['HTTPS'], auth: none, encrypted
  • Mobile App → API Gateway (—) — ['HTTPS'], auth: none, encrypted
⬆ Egress (data leaving this zone):
  • API Gateway → Auth Service (—) — ['HTTPS'], auth: none, encrypted
  • API Gateway → Orders Service (—) — ['HTTP'], auth: none, UNENCRYPTED

🛡 Application tier

3 components 2 ingress flows 1 egress flow 12 cross-boundary threats
Contains: Auth Service (service), Orders Service (service), Payment Provider (external)

  • API Gateway → Orders Service (—) — ['HTTP'], auth: none, UNENCRYPTED

  • Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) (—) — ['TCP'], auth: none, UNENCRYPTED

Findings (92)

These 92 findings are proven by your model. A further 133 standard checks — generic risks for these component types your model doesn’t yet confirm or rule out — are not counted as findings and stay hidden until you flip the “Standard checks” toggle.

All STRIDELINDDUNPASTA Off Hidden (133)
Broken access control / missing authz check
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · API Gateway (api) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk49/50 · Critical
Damage9/10
Reproducibility10/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability10/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Endpoints fail to verify the caller has permission for the action.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from an external user by following flows.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to API Gateway as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to API Gateway should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

A01API1

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Privilege transit across boundary: Mobile App → API Gateway
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · API Gateway (api) CWE-441 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk48/50 · Critical
Damage9/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability10/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Mobile App → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'DMZ (public-facing)' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Internet' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Mobile App → API Gateway crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Internet`.
  2. Issues requests to API Gateway that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `DMZ (public-facing)`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When API Gateway acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

🗺Framework mapping

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Admin Portal (webapp).

📝Description

Endpoints fail to verify the caller has permission for the action.

🔎Why this fired

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Admin Portal as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Admin Portal should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.

Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Auth Service (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Auth Service (service).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Application tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'DMZ (public-facing)' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Auth Service crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `DMZ (public-facing)`.
  2. Issues requests to Auth Service that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Application tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Auth Service acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

A01API1

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-441 — Confused Deputy ↗
Stored data tampering via injection
Critical STRIDE · Tampering · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk47/50 · Critical
Damage10/10
Reproducibility10/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

SQL/NoSQL/command injection alters records or schema.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from an external user by following flows.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Orders DB (Postgres) (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Orders DB (Postgres) processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗

  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.

Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) (label: *—*, protocol: TCP, auth: none, encrypted: no), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Data tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Application tier' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application tier`.
  2. Issues requests to Orders DB (Postgres) that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Data tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Orders DB (Postgres) acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Orders Service (label: *—*, protocol: HTTP, auth: none, encrypted: no), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Orders Service (service).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Application tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'DMZ (public-facing)' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Orders Service crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `DMZ (public-facing)`.
  2. Issues requests to Orders Service that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Application tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Orders Service acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'DMZ (public-facing)' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Internet' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Internet`.
  2. Issues requests to Web Frontend that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `DMZ (public-facing)`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Web Frontend acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

A01

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-441 — Confused Deputy ↗
Authentication bypass via credential stuffing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · API Gateway (api) CWE-287 standard check
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Attackers reuse leaked credentials to impersonate legitimate users.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates API Gateway's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. API Gateway accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Session token hijacking
High STRIDE · Spoofing · API Gateway (api) CWE-287 standard check
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Attacker steals or predicts a session token to impersonate a user.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates API Gateway's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. API Gateway accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
API key / service identity spoofing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · API Gateway (api) CWE-287 standard check

DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

An attacker obtains or forges an API key to act as a trusted service.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates API Gateway's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. API Gateway accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Log tampering
High STRIDE · Repudiation · API Gateway (api) CWE-778 standard check
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

An attacker with access modifies or deletes audit trails.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through API Gateway (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🗺Framework mapping

A09

🔗References

A09:2021 Security Logging & Monitoring Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Sensitive data exposure in transit
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · API Gateway (api) CWE-319 finding
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability10/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

PII, secrets, or tokens transmitted over unencrypted channels.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: a data flow touching this element is unencrypted.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches API Gateway via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-319 — Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information ↗
Sensitive data exposure at rest
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · API Gateway (api) CWE-200 standard check

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Stored PII / secrets accessible without proper authorization.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches API Gateway via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

User can access another user's resources by guessing IDs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from an external user by following flows.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches API Gateway via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02A01API1

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Application-layer DDoS
High STRIDE · Denial of Service · API Gateway (api) CWE-400 finding
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Attacker floods expensive endpoints (e.g., search, login).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from a less-trusted / public zone.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in API Gateway (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. API Gateway's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach API Gateway; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🗺Framework mapping

A05

🔗References

A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-400 — Uncontrolled Resource Consumption (DoS) ↗
Privilege escalation via mass assignment
High STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · API Gateway (api) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk42/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

User submits extra fields (e.g., role=admin) and they bind to the model.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from an external user by following flows.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to API Gateway as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to API Gateway should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

A01API1

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Unauthenticated flow: Mobile App → API Gateway
Critical STRIDE · Spoofing · API Gateway (api) CWE-306 finding High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk44/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Mobile App → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Mobile App → API Gateway declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at API Gateway (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. API Gateway processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🗺Framework mapping

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Web Frontend → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Web Frontend → API Gateway declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at API Gateway (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. API Gateway processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Admin Portal → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Admin Portal → API Gateway declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at API Gateway (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. API Gateway processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.

Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗
Trust-boundary crossing without strong authn: Mobile App → API Gateway
Critical STRIDE · Spoofing · API Gateway (api) CWE-287 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk43/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Mobile App → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Flow '' crosses trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Mobile App → API Gateway crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. API Gateway accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into API Gateway, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Cross-boundary input not validated: Mobile App → API Gateway
Critical STRIDE · Tampering · API Gateway (api) CWE-20 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk43/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Mobile App → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'DMZ (public-facing)' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Mobile App → API Gateway crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to API Gateway.
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. API Gateway processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into API Gateway or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever API Gateway does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🗺Framework mapping

A03

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-20 — Improper Input Validation ↗
Cross-boundary data exposure risk: Mobile App → API Gateway
Critical STRIDE · Information Disclosure · API Gateway (api) CWE-200 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk44/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability10/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Mobile App → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Information leaving 'Internet' into 'DMZ (public-facing)' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Mobile App → API Gateway crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Authentication bypass via credential stuffing

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Admin Portal (webapp).

📝Description

Attackers reuse leaked credentials to impersonate legitimate users.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Admin Portal's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Admin Portal accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Admin Portal (webapp).

📝Description

An attacker obtains or forges an API key to act as a trusted service.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Admin Portal's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Admin Portal accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Log tampering
High STRIDE · Repudiation · Admin Portal (webapp) CWE-778 standard check
▼
DREAD risk38/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Admin Portal (webapp).

📝Description

An attacker with access modifies or deletes audit trails.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Admin Portal (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🗺Framework mapping

A09

🔗References

A09:2021 Security Logging & Monitoring Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Sensitive data exposure in transit
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Admin Portal (webapp) CWE-319 standard check
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability10/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Admin Portal (webapp).

📝Description

PII, secrets, or tokens transmitted over unencrypted channels.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Admin Portal via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-319 — Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information ↗
Sensitive data exposure at rest
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Admin Portal (webapp) CWE-200 standard check

Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability10/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Admin Portal (webapp).

📝Description

Stored PII / secrets accessible without proper authorization.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Admin Portal via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Admin Portal (webapp).

📝Description

User can access another user's resources by guessing IDs.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Admin Portal via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

A02A01API1

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Application-layer DDoS
High STRIDE · Denial of Service · Admin Portal (webapp) CWE-400 finding
▼
DREAD risk38/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Admin Portal (webapp).

📝Description

Attacker floods expensive endpoints (e.g., search, login).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from a less-trusted / public zone.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in Admin Portal (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. Admin Portal's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach Admin Portal; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🗺Framework mapping

A05

🔗References

A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-400 — Uncontrolled Resource Consumption (DoS) ↗
Privilege escalation via mass assignment
High STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Admin Portal (webapp) CWE-269 standard check
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Admin Portal (webapp).

📝Description

User submits extra fields (e.g., role=admin) and they bind to the model.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Admin Portal as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Admin Portal should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

A01

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Unauthenticated flow: API Gateway → Auth Service
Critical STRIDE · Spoofing · Auth Service (service) CWE-306 finding High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Auth Service (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Auth Service (service).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Auth Service declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at Auth Service (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Auth Service processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🗺Framework mapping

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗
Trust-boundary crossing without strong authn: API Gateway → Auth Service
Critical STRIDE · Spoofing · Auth Service (service) CWE-287 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Auth Service (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Auth Service (service).

📝Description

Flow '' crosses trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Auth Service crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. Auth Service accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into Auth Service, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Cross-boundary input not validated: API Gateway → Auth Service
Critical STRIDE · Tampering · Auth Service (service) CWE-20 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Auth Service (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Auth Service (service).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'Application tier' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Auth Service crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Auth Service.
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. Auth Service processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into Auth Service or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Auth Service does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🗺Framework mapping

A03

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-20 — Improper Input Validation ↗
Cross-boundary data exposure risk: API Gateway → Auth Service
Critical STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Auth Service (service) CWE-200 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability10/10
Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Auth Service (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Auth Service (service).

📝Description

Information leaving 'DMZ (public-facing)' into 'Application tier' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Auth Service crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Authentication bypass via credential stuffing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Customer Browser (user) CWE-287 standard check
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Customer Browser (user).

📝Description

Attackers reuse leaked credentials to impersonate legitimate users.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'user' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker obtains valid credentials via phishing, credential stuffing, or a third-party breach.
  2. Logs in as the legitimate user/entity at Customer Browser.
  3. Performs actions indistinguishable from the real user (auth events look normal).
  4. Damage scales with the impersonated user's permissions; admin accounts cause the most harm.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🗺Framework mapping

🔗References

Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Attacker on the network path alters request/response payloads.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: a data flow touching this element is unencrypted.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Orders DB (Postgres) (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Orders DB (Postgres) processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Config / secret tampering

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Unauthorized changes to runtime config alter security behavior.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'database' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Orders DB (Postgres) (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Orders DB (Postgres) processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

A03

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Log tampering
High STRIDE · Repudiation · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-778 standard check
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

An attacker with access modifies or deletes audit trails.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'database' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Orders DB (Postgres) (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🗺Framework mapping

A09

🔗References

A09:2021 Security Logging & Monitoring Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Sensitive data exposure in transit
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

PII, secrets, or tokens transmitted over unencrypted channels.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: a data flow touching this element is unencrypted.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Orders DB (Postgres) via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For database, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗

Within component Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Stored PII / secrets accessible without proper authorization.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is a data store.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Orders DB (Postgres) via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For database, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

User can access another user's resources by guessing IDs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from an external user by following flows.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Orders DB (Postgres) via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For database, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.
