▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Attacker floods expensive endpoints (e.g., search, login).

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'database' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in Orders DB (Postgres) (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. Orders DB (Postgres)'s resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach Orders DB (Postgres); cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🗺Framework mapping

A05

🔗References

A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-400 — Uncontrolled Resource Consumption (DoS) ↗
Privilege escalation via mass assignment
High STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk37/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

User submits extra fields (e.g., role=admin) and they bind to the model.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from an external user by following flows.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Orders DB (Postgres) as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Orders DB (Postgres) should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

A01

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Unencrypted flow: Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres)
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-319 finding
▼
DREAD risk42/50 · Critical
Damage8/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) (label: *—*, protocol: TCP, auth: none, encrypted: no). The receiving component is Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Data flow '' uses TCP without encryption.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) declares encrypted=no (protocol TCP).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker gains read access to the network path (sniffing on shared LAN, compromised router, hostile cloud tenant, malicious admin).
  2. Captures cleartext traffic on the TCP channel.
  3. Extracts credentials, session tokens, PII, or business secrets from packet captures.
  4. Uses the credentials to impersonate Orders DB (Postgres) or the calling component, or sells/leaks the captured data.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enable TLS 1.3 (or 1.2 with strong ciphers) on the flow
Replace plain ['TCP'] with its TLS variant. For databases use TLS-enabled drivers; for queues like AMQP/Kafka, configure broker certs and require client TLS.
preventive
Enforce certificate validation
Verify hostname, validate the chain to a known CA, pin certificates or use a private CA for internal services. Disable cipher fallbacks to NULL/EXPORT/RC4.
detective
Scan for cleartext fallbacks
Add a network-policy / NetworkPolicy / security-group rule that drops any traffic on the cleartext port. Alert if it ever fires.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-319 — Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information ↗
Unauthenticated flow: Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres)
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-306 finding
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage8/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) (label: *—*, protocol: TCP, auth: none, encrypted: no). The receiving component is Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at Orders DB (Postgres) (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Orders DB (Postgres) processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🗺Framework mapping

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗
Trust-boundary crossing without strong authn: Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres)
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-287 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage8/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability8/10
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) (label: *—*, protocol: TCP, auth: none, encrypted: no), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Flow '' crosses trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. Orders DB (Postgres) accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into Orders DB (Postgres), terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Cross-boundary input not validated: Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres)
High STRIDE · Tampering · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-20 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage8/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability8/10
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) (label: *—*, protocol: TCP, auth: none, encrypted: no), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'Data tier' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Orders DB (Postgres).
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. Orders DB (Postgres) processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into Orders DB (Postgres) or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Orders DB (Postgres) does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🗺Framework mapping

A03

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-20 — Improper Input Validation ↗
Cross-boundary data exposure risk: Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres)
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-200 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk42/50 · Critical
Damage8/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) (label: *—*, protocol: TCP, auth: none, encrypted: no), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Information leaving 'Application tier' into 'Data tier' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Orders Service (label: *—*, protocol: HTTP, auth: none, encrypted: no). The receiving component is Orders Service (service).

📝Description

Data flow '' uses HTTP without encryption.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Orders Service declares encrypted=no (protocol HTTP). · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker gains read access to the network path (sniffing on shared LAN, compromised router, hostile cloud tenant, malicious admin).
  2. Captures cleartext traffic on the HTTP channel.
  3. Extracts credentials, session tokens, PII, or business secrets from packet captures.
  4. Uses the credentials to impersonate Orders Service or the calling component, or sells/leaks the captured data.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enable TLS 1.3 (or 1.2 with strong ciphers) on the flow
Replace plain ['HTTP'] with its TLS variant. For databases use TLS-enabled drivers; for queues like AMQP/Kafka, configure broker certs and require client TLS.
preventive
Enforce certificate validation
Verify hostname, validate the chain to a known CA, pin certificates or use a private CA for internal services. Disable cipher fallbacks to NULL/EXPORT/RC4.
detective
Scan for cleartext fallbacks
Add a network-policy / NetworkPolicy / security-group rule that drops any traffic on the cleartext port. Alert if it ever fires.

⚔️Attack scenario

  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Orders Service processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.

Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Orders Service (label: *—*, protocol: HTTP, auth: none, encrypted: no), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Orders Service (service).

📝Description

Flow '' crosses trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Orders Service crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. Orders Service accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into Orders Service, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Cross-boundary input not validated: API Gateway → Orders Service
Critical STRIDE · Tampering · Orders Service (service) CWE-20 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk42/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability10/10
Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Orders Service (label: *—*, protocol: HTTP, auth: none, encrypted: no), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Orders Service (service).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'Application tier' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Orders Service crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Orders Service.
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. Orders Service processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into Orders Service or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Orders Service does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🗺Framework mapping

A03

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-20 — Improper Input Validation ↗
Cross-boundary data exposure risk: API Gateway → Orders Service
Critical STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Orders Service (service) CWE-200 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk42/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability10/10
Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Orders Service (label: *—*, protocol: HTTP, auth: none, encrypted: no), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Orders Service (service).

📝Description

Information leaving 'DMZ (public-facing)' into 'Application tier' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Orders Service crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Unauthenticated flow: Orders Service → Payment Provider
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Payment Provider (external) CWE-306 finding
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability8/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Payment Provider (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Payment Provider (external).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Orders Service → Payment Provider declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at Payment Provider (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Payment Provider processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🗺Framework mapping

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗
Authentication bypass via credential stuffing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-287 standard check
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Attackers reuse leaked credentials to impersonate legitimate users.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Web Frontend's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Web Frontend accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Session token hijacking
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-287 standard check
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Attacker steals or predicts a session token to impersonate a user.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Web Frontend's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Web Frontend accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
API key / service identity spoofing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-287 standard check
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

An attacker obtains or forges an API key to act as a trusted service.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Web Frontend's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Web Frontend accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Log tampering
High STRIDE · Repudiation · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-778 standard check
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

An attacker with access modifies or deletes audit trails.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Web Frontend (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🗺Framework mapping

A09

🔗References

A09:2021 Security Logging & Monitoring Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Sensitive data exposure in transit
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-319 standard check
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability10/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

PII, secrets, or tokens transmitted over unencrypted channels.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Web Frontend via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-319 — Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information ↗

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Stored PII / secrets accessible without proper authorization.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Web Frontend via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

User can access another user's resources by guessing IDs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from an external user by following flows.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Web Frontend via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

A02A01API1

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Application-layer DDoS
High STRIDE · Denial of Service · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-400 finding
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Attacker floods expensive endpoints (e.g., search, login).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from a less-trusted / public zone.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in Web Frontend (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. Web Frontend's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach Web Frontend; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🗺Framework mapping

A05

🔗References

A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-400 — Uncontrolled Resource Consumption (DoS) ↗
Privilege escalation via mass assignment
High STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

User submits extra fields (e.g., role=admin) and they bind to the model.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from an external user by following flows.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Web Frontend as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Web Frontend should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

A01

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at Web Frontend (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Web Frontend processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗
Trust-boundary crossing without strong authn: Customer Browser → Web Frontend
Critical STRIDE · Spoofing · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-287 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk43/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Flow '' crosses trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. Web Frontend accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into Web Frontend, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Cross-boundary input not validated: Customer Browser → Web Frontend

Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'DMZ (public-facing)' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Web Frontend.
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. Web Frontend processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into Web Frontend or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Web Frontend does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Information leaving 'Internet' into 'DMZ (public-facing)' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Insufficient audit logging
Medium STRIDE · Repudiation · API Gateway (api) CWE-778 standard check
▼
DREAD risk30/50 · High
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Critical user actions cannot be reliably attributed after the fact.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through API Gateway (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🗺Framework mapping

A09

🔗References

A09:2021 Security Logging & Monitoring Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Verbose error messages / stack traces
Medium STRIDE · Information Disclosure · API Gateway (api) CWE-200 standard check
▼
DREAD risk31/50 · High
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Error responses leak implementation details to attackers.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches API Gateway via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Resource exhaustion via unbounded input
Medium STRIDE · Denial of Service · API Gateway (api) CWE-400 finding
▼
DREAD risk30/50 · High
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Large payloads or unbounded loops exhaust CPU/memory.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from a less-trusted / public zone.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in API Gateway (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. API Gateway's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach API Gateway; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🗺Framework mapping

A05LLM10T4

🔗References

A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration ↗LLM10:2025 Unbounded Consumption ↗Agentic T4 — Resource Overload ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-400 — Uncontrolled Resource Consumption (DoS) ↗
Algorithmic complexity attack
Medium STRIDE · Denial of Service · API Gateway (api) CWE-400 finding
▼
DREAD risk30/50 · High
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Crafted inputs trigger worst-case algorithm behavior (e.g., regex DoS).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from a less-trusted / public zone.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in API Gateway (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. API Gateway's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach API Gateway; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🗺Framework mapping

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

🔎Why this fired

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
GDPR / CCPA gaps for personal data
High LINDDUN · Non-compliance · API Gateway (api) CWE-778 standard check
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Personal data (PII) triggers GDPR / CCPA duties: lawful basis, DSAR handling, retention limits, and data-residency controls.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes API Gateway via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around API Gateway. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for api components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

📝Description

Protected health information triggers HIPAA duties: BAAs, minimum-necessary access, audit controls, and breach notification.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes API Gateway via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

preventive

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Cardholder data triggers PCI-DSS: scope segmentation, tokenization / no-store of PANs, and key management.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes API Gateway via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around API Gateway. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for api components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Unauthenticated flow: Mobile App → API Gateway
Critical LINDDUN · Stage 3 — Application Decomposition · API Gateway (api) CWE-306 finding High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk44/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Mobile App → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Mobile App → API Gateway declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at API Gateway (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. API Gateway processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🗺Framework mapping

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Web Frontend → API Gateway declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at API Gateway (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. API Gateway processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

▼
DREAD risk44/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Admin Portal → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Admin Portal → API Gateway declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at API Gateway (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. API Gateway processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗
Cross-boundary PII transfer: Mobile App → API Gateway
Critical LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · API Gateway (api) CWE-200 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk43/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Mobile App → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Personal data crossing trust boundaries triggers data-protection obligations (purpose, consent, residency, processor agreements).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Mobile App → API Gateway crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches API Gateway via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Third-party data leakage
High LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Admin Portal (webapp) CWE-200 standard check
▼

Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Admin Portal (webapp).

📝Description

PII shared with analytics/ad SDKs without consent.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Admin Portal via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'user' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Mobile App via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (user).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Mobile App. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for user components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Mobile App (user).

📝Description

Cardholder data triggers PCI-DSS: scope segmentation, tokenization / no-store of PANs, and key management.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'user' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Mobile App via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (user).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

preventive

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Re-identification of anonymized data
High LINDDUN · Identifiability · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-359 finding
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Quasi-identifiers (zip, DOB, gender) re-identify users in 'anonymous' exports.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is tagged as handling sensitive data.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker collects records emitted by Orders DB (Postgres) (logs, events, telemetry, public outputs).
  2. Uses quasi-identifiers (timestamps, IPs, device fingerprints, behavioral patterns) to link multiple records to the same individual.
  3. Builds a behavior profile that reveals identity even though no single record contains an explicit identifier.
  4. Privacy violation — re-identification, surveillance, or unauthorized profiling.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Orders DB (Postgres). Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for database components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-359 — Privacy Violation ↗
Third-party data leakage
High LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

PII shared with analytics/ad SDKs without consent.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is tagged as handling sensitive data.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Orders DB (Postgres) via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For database, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗

Within component Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Personal data (PII) triggers GDPR / CCPA duties: lawful basis, DSAR handling, retention limits, and data-residency controls.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is tagged as handling PII.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Orders DB (Postgres) via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (database).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

preventive

📝Description

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Cardholder data triggers PCI-DSS: scope segmentation, tokenization / no-store of PANs, and key management.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'database' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Orders DB (Postgres) via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (database).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Orders DB (Postgres). Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for database components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Unencrypted flow: Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres)
High LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-319 finding
▼
DREAD risk42/50 · Critical
Damage8/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) (label: *—*, protocol: TCP, auth: none, encrypted: no). The receiving component is Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Data flow '' uses TCP without encryption.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) declares encrypted=no (protocol TCP).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker gains read access to the network path (sniffing on shared LAN, compromised router, hostile cloud tenant, malicious admin).
  2. Captures cleartext traffic on the TCP channel.
  3. Extracts credentials, session tokens, PII, or business secrets from packet captures.
  4. Uses the credentials to impersonate Orders DB (Postgres) or the calling component, or sells/leaks the captured data.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enable TLS 1.3 (or 1.2 with strong ciphers) on the flow
Replace plain ['TCP'] with its TLS variant. For databases use TLS-enabled drivers; for queues like AMQP/Kafka, configure broker certs and require client TLS.
preventive
Enforce certificate validation
Verify hostname, validate the chain to a known CA, pin certificates or use a private CA for internal services. Disable cipher fallbacks to NULL/EXPORT/RC4.
detective
Scan for cleartext fallbacks
Add a network-policy / NetworkPolicy / security-group rule that drops any traffic on the cleartext port. Alert if it ever fires.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-319 — Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information ↗
Unauthenticated flow: Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres)
High LINDDUN · Stage 3 — Application Decomposition · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-306 finding
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage8/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) (label: *—*, protocol: TCP, auth: none, encrypted: no). The receiving component is Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at Orders DB (Postgres) (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Orders DB (Postgres) processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🗺Framework mapping

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗
Cross-boundary PII transfer: Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres)
High LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-200 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage8/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability8/10
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) (label: *—*, protocol: TCP, auth: none, encrypted: no), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Personal data crossing trust boundaries triggers data-protection obligations (purpose, consent, residency, processor agreements).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Orders DB (Postgres) via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For database, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

Data flow '' uses HTTP without encryption.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Orders Service declares encrypted=no (protocol HTTP). · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker gains read access to the network path (sniffing on shared LAN, compromised router, hostile cloud tenant, malicious admin).
  2. Captures cleartext traffic on the HTTP channel.
  3. Extracts credentials, session tokens, PII, or business secrets from packet captures.
  4. Uses the credentials to impersonate Orders Service or the calling component, or sells/leaks the captured data.

preventive
Enable TLS 1.3 (or 1.2 with strong ciphers) on the flow
Replace plain ['HTTP'] with its TLS variant. For databases use TLS-enabled drivers; for queues like AMQP/Kafka, configure broker certs and require client TLS.
preventive
Enforce certificate validation
Verify hostname, validate the chain to a known CA, pin certificates or use a private CA for internal services. Disable cipher fallbacks to NULL/EXPORT/RC4.
detective
Scan for cleartext fallbacks
Add a network-policy / NetworkPolicy / security-group rule that drops any traffic on the cleartext port. Alert if it ever fires.
