Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Payment Provider (external).

📝Description

Cardholder data triggers PCI-DSS: scope segmentation, tokenization / no-store of PANs, and key management.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is tagged as handling cardholder data.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Payment Provider via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (external).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Payment Provider. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for external components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Unauthenticated flow: Orders Service → Payment Provider
High LINDDUN · Stage 3 — Application Decomposition · Payment Provider (external) CWE-306 finding
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability8/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Payment Provider (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Payment Provider (external).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Orders Service → Payment Provider declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at Payment Provider (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Payment Provider processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🗺Framework mapping

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗
Third-party data leakage
High LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-200 standard check
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

PII shared with analytics/ad SDKs without consent.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Web Frontend via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Personal data (PII) triggers GDPR / CCPA duties: lawful basis, DSAR handling, retention limits, and data-residency controls.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Web Frontend via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (webapp).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Web Frontend. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for webapp components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

Unauthenticated flow: Customer Browser → Web Frontend
Critical LINDDUN · Stage 3 — Application Decomposition · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-306 finding High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk43/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at Web Frontend (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Web Frontend processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🗺Framework mapping

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗
Cross-boundary PII transfer: Customer Browser → Web Frontend
Critical LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-200 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk43/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Personal data crossing trust boundaries triggers data-protection obligations (purpose, consent, residency, processor agreements).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Web Frontend via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Cross-service user linkability
Medium LINDDUN · Linkability · API Gateway (api) CWE-359 standard check
▼
DREAD risk30/50 · High
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Identifiers (emails, device IDs) let separate datasets be joined to profile a user.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker collects records emitted by API Gateway (logs, events, telemetry, public outputs).
  2. Uses quasi-identifiers (timestamps, IPs, device fingerprints, behavioral patterns) to link multiple records to the same individual.
  3. Builds a behavior profile that reveals identity even though no single record contains an explicit identifier.
  4. Privacy violation — re-identification, surveillance, or unauthorized profiling.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around API Gateway. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for api components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-359 — Privacy Violation ↗
Account enumeration via differential responses
Medium LINDDUN · Detectability · API Gateway (api) CWE-359 finding
▼
DREAD risk31/50 · High
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Login / signup / password reset reveal whether an account exists.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from an external user by following flows.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes API Gateway via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Detectability).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around API Gateway. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for api components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

📝Description

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches API Gateway via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses

preventive

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-359 — Privacy Violation ↗
Excessive data collection
Medium LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-200 standard check
▼
DREAD risk29/50 · Medium
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

System collects more PII than needed for its purpose.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Web Frontend via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Lack of transparent privacy notice
Medium LINDDUN · Unawareness · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-359 standard check
▼
DREAD risk29/50 · Medium
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Users don't know what data is collected or for what purpose.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Web Frontend via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Unawareness).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (webapp).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Web Frontend. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for webapp components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-359 — Privacy Violation ↗
Forced attribution of sensitive actions
Low LINDDUN · Non-repudiation · API Gateway (api) CWE-778 standard check
▼
DREAD risk20/50 · Medium
Damage3/10
Reproducibility2/10
Exploitability5/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability5/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

User cannot deny a sensitive action even when they should have plausible deniability.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through API Gateway (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🗺Framework mapping

A09

🔗References

A09:2021 Security Logging & Monitoring Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Forced attribution of sensitive actions
Low LINDDUN · Non-repudiation · Admin Portal (webapp) CWE-778 standard check

📝Description

User cannot deny a sensitive action even when they should have plausible deniability.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Admin Portal (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

📝Description

User cannot deny a sensitive action even when they should have plausible deniability.

🔎Why this fired

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Web Frontend (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).

A09

🔗References

A09:2021 Security Logging & Monitoring Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Unauthenticated flow: Mobile App → API Gateway
Critical PASTA · Stage 3 — Application Decomposition · API Gateway (api) CWE-306 finding High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk44/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Mobile App → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Mobile App → API Gateway declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at API Gateway (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. API Gateway processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🗺Framework mapping

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗PASTA reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗

On the data flow Web Frontend → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Web Frontend → API Gateway declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at API Gateway (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. API Gateway processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

On the data flow Admin Portal → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Admin Portal → API Gateway declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at API Gateway (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. API Gateway processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive

Implicit trust across decomposition boundary: Mobile App → API Gateway
Critical PASTA · Stage 3 — Application Decomposition · API Gateway (api) CWE-693 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk43/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Mobile App → API Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is API Gateway (api).

📝Description

Decomposition mapped a boundary between 'Internet' and 'DMZ (public-facing)' but implicit trust persists across it.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Mobile App → API Gateway crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes API Gateway via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 3 — Application Decomposition).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around API Gateway. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for api components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

PASTA reference ↗CWE-693 — Protection Mechanism Failure ↗

Affected users5/10
Discoverability9/10

On the data flow API Gateway → Auth Service (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Auth Service (service).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Auth Service declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at Auth Service (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Auth Service processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.

Rate-limit unauthenticated probes

Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Auth Service (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Auth Service (service).

📝Description

Decomposition mapped a boundary between 'DMZ (public-facing)' and 'Application tier' but implicit trust persists across it.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Auth Service crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Auth Service via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 3 — Application Decomposition).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (service).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive

detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators

🔗References

PASTA reference ↗CWE-693 — Protection Mechanism Failure ↗
Unencrypted flow: Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres)
High PASTA · Disclosure of information · Orders DB (Postgres) (database) CWE-319 finding
▼
DREAD risk42/50 · Critical
Damage8/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) (label: *—*, protocol: TCP, auth: none, encrypted: no). The receiving component is Orders DB (Postgres) (database).

📝Description

Data flow '' uses TCP without encryption.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Orders Service → Orders DB (Postgres) declares encrypted=no (protocol TCP).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker gains read access to the network path (sniffing on shared LAN, compromised router, hostile cloud tenant, malicious admin).
  2. Captures cleartext traffic on the TCP channel.
  3. Extracts credentials, session tokens, PII, or business secrets from packet captures.
  4. Uses the credentials to impersonate Orders DB (Postgres) or the calling component, or sells/leaks the captured data.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enable TLS 1.3 (or 1.2 with strong ciphers) on the flow
Replace plain ['TCP'] with its TLS variant. For databases use TLS-enabled drivers; for queues like AMQP/Kafka, configure broker certs and require client TLS.
preventive
Enforce certificate validation
Verify hostname, validate the chain to a known CA, pin certificates or use a private CA for internal services. Disable cipher fallbacks to NULL/EXPORT/RC4.
detective
Scan for cleartext fallbacks
Add a network-policy / NetworkPolicy / security-group rule that drops any traffic on the cleartext port. Alert if it ever fires.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗PASTA reference ↗CWE-319 — Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information ↗

  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Orders DB (Postgres) processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Orders Service declares encrypted=no (protocol HTTP). · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker gains read access to the network path (sniffing on shared LAN, compromised router, hostile cloud tenant, malicious admin).
  2. Captures cleartext traffic on the HTTP channel.
  3. Extracts credentials, session tokens, PII, or business secrets from packet captures.
  4. Uses the credentials to impersonate Orders Service or the calling component, or sells/leaks the captured data.

Enable TLS 1.3 (or 1.2 with strong ciphers) on the flow
Replace plain ['HTTP'] with its TLS variant. For databases use TLS-enabled drivers; for queues like AMQP/Kafka, configure broker certs and require client TLS.

Enforce certificate validation
Verify hostname, validate the chain to a known CA, pin certificates or use a private CA for internal services. Disable cipher fallbacks to NULL/EXPORT/RC4.
detective
Scan for cleartext fallbacks
Add a network-policy / NetworkPolicy / security-group rule that drops any traffic on the cleartext port. Alert if it ever fires.

Evidenced: flow API Gateway → Orders Service crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Orders Service via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 3 — Application Decomposition).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (service).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Orders Service. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for service components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

PASTA reference ↗CWE-693 — Protection Mechanism Failure ↗
Unauthenticated flow: Orders Service → Payment Provider
High PASTA · Stage 3 — Application Decomposition · Payment Provider (external) CWE-306 finding
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability8/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Payment Provider (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Payment Provider (external).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Orders Service → Payment Provider declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at Payment Provider (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Payment Provider processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🗺Framework mapping

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗PASTA reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗
Unauthenticated flow: Customer Browser → Web Frontend
Critical PASTA · Stage 3 — Application Decomposition · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-306 finding High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk43/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility9/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend declares auth=none — no strong caller authentication. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at Web Frontend (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Web Frontend processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🗺Framework mapping

A07API2

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗API2:2023 Broken Authentication ↗PASTA reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗
Implicit trust across decomposition boundary: Customer Browser → Web Frontend
Critical PASTA · Stage 3 — Application Decomposition · Web Frontend (webapp) CWE-693 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk43/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility7/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is Web Frontend (webapp).

📝Description

Decomposition mapped a boundary between 'Internet' and 'DMZ (public-facing)' but implicit trust persists across it.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer Browser → Web Frontend crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with no caller authentication

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Web Frontend via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 3 — Application Decomposition).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (webapp).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Web Frontend. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for webapp components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

PASTA reference ↗CWE-693 — Protection Mechanism Failure ↗
Manual step — map business impact & asset owners
Info PASTA · Stage 1 — Business Objectives · API Gateway (api) CWE-20 standard check
▼
DREAD risk15/50 · Low
Damage2/10
Reproducibility1/10
Exploitability4/10
Affected users4/10
Discoverability4/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 1 requires defining business objectives and impact tiers. The tool does NOT determine this automatically: assign an owner and a business-impact tier to each component, and record revenue/regulatory exposure.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes API Gateway via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 1 — Business Objectives).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around API Gateway. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for api components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

📝Description

PASTA Stage 2 requires a dependency/SBOM inventory and CVE analysis. The tool does NOT scan dependencies: run SCA (e.g. dependabot/trivy) and attach the SBOM to complete this stage.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api).

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 4 requires profiling plausible adversaries and their techniques. The tool does NOT infer threat actors: map actor tiers (script-kiddie → nation-state) and relevant MITRE ATT&CK TTPs to your surfaces.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

Info PASTA · Stage 6 — Attack Modeling · API Gateway (api) CWE-20 standard check
▼

Damage2/10
Reproducibility1/10
Exploitability4/10
Affected users4/10
Discoverability4/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 6 requires constructing attack trees. The tool does NOT generate attack trees: model plausible kill-chains (e.g. phish → token theft → admin) for the highest-risk flows and rank them by likelihood × impact.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes API Gateway via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 6 — Attack Modeling).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

Discoverability4/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Auth Service (service).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 4 requires profiling plausible adversaries and their techniques. The tool does NOT infer threat actors: map actor tiers (script-kiddie → nation-state) and relevant MITRE ATT&CK TTPs to your surfaces.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'service' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Auth Service via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 4 — Threat Analysis).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (service).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Auth Service. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for service components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Auth Service (service).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 6 requires constructing attack trees. The tool does NOT generate attack trees: model plausible kill-chains (e.g. phish → token theft → admin) for the highest-risk flows and rank them by likelihood × impact.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'service' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (service).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

Baseline check for a 'user' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 2 — Technical Scope).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (service).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Orders Service. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for service components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

PASTA Stage 6 requires constructing attack trees. The tool does NOT generate attack trees: model plausible kill-chains (e.g. phish → token theft → admin) for the highest-risk flows and rank them by likelihood × impact.

PASTA Stage 7 quantifies and prioritizes risk. The tool provides severity and DREAD scores per threat as inputs, but does NOT record risk decisions: track each risk to an explicit accept/mitigate/transfer/avoid decision with an owner.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Payment Provider (external).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 1 requires defining business objectives and impact tiers. The tool does NOT determine this automatically: assign an owner and a business-impact tier to each component, and record revenue/regulatory exposure.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'external' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Payment Provider via reachable inputs.
